content:
# 2 Corinthians Chapter 11 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

---

### v.2 — "jealousy of God" vs. "divine jealousy" vs. "jealousy of God himself"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I am jealous for you with a jealousy of God" | theou zeloi — genitive; God's own jealousy |
| LOGOS | "I feel a divine jealousy for you" | Adjective form |
| PNEUMA | "I am jealous for you with the jealousy of God himself" | Adds "himself" for emphasis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Divine jealousy" captures theou zeloi concisely. PNEUMA's "God himself" adds emphasis not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor addition by PNEUMA.

---

### v.3 — "Eua" vs. "Eve" / "simplicity and purity" vs. "sincere and pure devotion"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the serpent deceived Eua...the simplicity and the purity toward the Christos" | Name transliterated; haplotetos kai hagnotetos |
| LOGOS | "the serpent deceived Eve...a sincere and pure devotion to Christ" | Standard name; "devotion" added for clarity |
| PNEUMA | "Eve was deceived by the cunning of the serpent...pure and simple devotion to Christ" | Reverses word order; adds "devotion" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. haplotetos ("simplicity/sincerity") and hagnotetos ("purity") are the key terms. Adding "devotion" clarifies the phrase in English. PNEUMA reverses the order but otherwise aligns.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor structural differences.

---

### v.4 — "you bear it well" vs. "you put up with it readily enough" vs. "you happily put up with whatever anyone tells you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you bear [it] well" | kalos anechesthe — "you bear well" |
| LOGOS | "you put up with it readily enough" | Idiomatic; captures the irony |
| PNEUMA | "You happily put up with whatever anyone tells you, even if..." | "Happily" and "whatever anyone tells you" — added |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The sarcasm of kalos ("well/fine") is captured by "readily enough." PNEUMA adds "happily" and "whatever anyone tells you" — making the sarcasm heavier than Paul's understated irony.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA over-explains the sarcasm.

---

### v.5 — "super-apostles" — rendering of hyperlian apostoloi

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the super-apostles" | hyperlian as compound "super-" |
| LOGOS | "these super-apostles" | Same; demonstrative added |
| PNEUMA | "these 'super apostles' who teach such things" | Scare quotes; adds "who teach such things" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "who teach such things" — not in the Greek. The scare quotes are also an editorial choice.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds interpretive gloss.

---

### v.8 — "robbed other assemblies" vs. "robbed other churches" vs. "'robbed' other churches"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Other assemblies I robbed" | esylesa as "robbed"; no quotation marks |
| LOGOS | "I robbed other churches" | Same; direct |
| PNEUMA | "I 'robbed' other churches" | Scare quotes around "robbed" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul's use of "robbed" (sylan — to strip/plunder) is deliberately provocative and ironic. Adding scare quotes (PNEUMA) signals the irony to the reader, but Paul's text works without them — the irony is in the word itself.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Editorial quotation marks not needed.

---

### v.15 — "their end will correspond to their deeds" vs. "they will get the punishment their wicked deeds deserve"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "whose end will be according to the works of them" | Literal kata ta erga auton |
| LOGOS | "Their end will correspond to their deeds" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "In the end they will get the punishment their wicked deeds deserve" | Adds "punishment" and "wicked" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul says their "end" (telos) will match their "works" (erga). PNEUMA adds "punishment" (not in Greek — telos is "end/outcome," not specifically punishment) and "wicked" (not in Greek — Paul lets the reader infer the moral quality).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds two words not in the text.

---

### v.20 — "puts on airs" vs. "exalts himself" vs. "takes control of everything"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if someone exalts himself" | epairetai — "lifts himself up" |
| LOGOS | "puts on airs" | Idiomatic |
| PNEUMA | "takes control of everything" | Interprets self-exaltation as domination |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. epairetai means "lifts oneself up/puts on airs." PNEUMA's "takes control of everything" adds "of everything" and shifts the meaning from arrogance to domination.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA changes the sense.

---

### v.24 — "from Ioudaioi" vs. "at the hands of the Jews" vs. "the Jewish leaders"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "From Ioudaioi five times I received forty [lashes] less one" | Transliterated; "from" |
| LOGOS | "Five times I received at the hands of the Jews the forty lashes less one" | "At the hands of the Jews" |
| PNEUMA | "Five different times the Jewish leaders gave me thirty-nine lashes" | Adds "leaders"; "thirty-nine" for "forty less one" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "leaders" — not in the text. The punishment was administered by synagogue authorities, but Paul says simply "Jews" (Ioudaioi). Also, "forty less one" is a specific legal formula (Deut 25:3 limits lashes to 40; Jewish practice stopped at 39 as a safeguard). "Thirty-nine" loses this legal background.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Forty lashes less one" preserves the legal formula.

---

### v.29 — "I am not indignant" vs. "I do not burn" vs. "I do not burn with anger"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Who is caused to stumble, and I do not burn?" | pyroumai as "burn" — open to interpretation |
| LOGOS | "Who is made to fall, and I am not indignant?" | "Indignant" — interprets the burning |
| PNEUMA | "Who is led astray, and I do not burn with anger?" | "Burn with anger" — specifies |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. pyroumai ("I burn") is deliberately ambiguous — Paul may burn with anger, shame, grief, or empathetic suffering. "Indignant" captures the most likely sense. PNEUMA adds "with anger" to specify what Paul leaves open.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. pyroumai is intentionally ambiguous.

---

### vv.32-33 — "governor under Aretas" vs. "ordered guards to watch the city gates"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the governor under Aretas the king was guarding the city of [the] Damaskenes to seize me" | Literal; ethnarch guarding city |
| LOGOS | "the governor under King Aretas was guarding the city of Damascus in order to seize me" | Same content; formal |
| PNEUMA | "the governor under King Aretas ordered guards to watch the city gates to catch me" | Adds "guards" and "city gates" — not in text |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "guards" and "city gates" — the text says the governor was "guarding" (ephrourei) the city, not specifically posting guards at gates.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds tactical details not in the text.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 5 | "Leaders," "wicked," "punishment," "city gates," "whatever anyone tells you" |
| PNEUMA over-explaining irony | 2 | "Happily," scare quotes around "robbed" |
| PNEUMA specifying ambiguity | 2 | "Burn with anger," "thirty-nine" for "forty less one" |
| EMET transliteration | 2 | "Eua," "Ioudaioi" |
| Legal formula preservation | 1 | "Forty lashes less one" vs. "thirty-nine" |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 11's fool's speech depends on irony, understatement, and deliberate rhetorical subversion. PNEUMA's tendency to make irony explicit, add editorial quotation marks, and specify Paul's intentional ambiguities works against Paul's rhetorical technique. The consensus preserves the original's sharp understatement and lets readers encounter the irony as Paul's audience did.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
